events.text: The patient is a 63-year-old female named Margaret Yuen. She is of East Asian (Cantonese) descent, born in Hong Kong, immigrated to the United States at age 32. She is a former smoker with a 12 pack-year history, having quit 18 years prior to diagnosis. She works as a high school math teacher. She has a family history of lung cancer in a paternal uncle. Her Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status is 1 at time of diagnosis.

At age 63 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IIIB (T3 N3 M0, AJCC 8th edition) non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma histology (ICD-O-3 histology code 8140/3), primary site: upper lobe of right lung (ICD-O-3 site code C34.1). TNM staging: T3 (tumor 5.8 cm with invasion of the parietal pleura), N3 (ipsilateral supraclavicular lymph node involvement and contralateral mediastinal involvement on PET-CT), M0 (no distant metastases at diagnosis). Summary stage: Regional. Relevant biomarkers: EGFR L858R mutation detected in exon 21 (allele frequency 38.2%); PD-L1 TPS 5% (Dako 22C3); ALK by IHC negative; ROS1 by FISH negative. Confirmation: Positive histology via CT-guided transthoracic needle biopsy of primary mass. Tumor size: 58 mm. Laterality: Right. Tumor location: Right upper lobe, abutting chest wall. Pleural effusion: None. Histologic grade: Moderately differentiated (Grade 2). Lymphovascular invasion: Present.

At age 63 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY from CT-guided core needle biopsy of right upper lobe mass (3 cores submitted). Microscopic description: Cores show infiltrating adenocarcinoma with acinar and lepidic growth patterns. Tumor cells are columnar with moderate nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, and moderate pale cytoplasm. No squamous or small cell differentiation identified. Immunohistochemistry: TTF-1 positive (diffuse, strong), Napsin-A positive, CK7 positive, CK20 negative, p40 negative, Synaptophysin negative, Chromogranin negative. Mucin stain: focally positive. EGFR by PCR (cobas): EGFR L858R mutation detected (exon 21). ALK IHC (D5F3): Negative. ROS1 IHC: Negative. PD-L1 22C3: TPS 5%. Final diagnosis: Invasive adenocarcinoma, right upper lobe, with EGFR L858R mutation.

At age 63 years, the patient had a PET-CT (whole body with diagnostic CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) performed for initial staging. Findings: Right upper lobe mass measuring 5.8 x 4.2 cm, hypermetabolic (SUVmax 14.3). Right hilar lymph nodes enlarged, hypermetabolic (SUVmax 7.8, up to 1.9 cm short axis). Right paratracheal lymph nodes (4R) hypermetabolic (SUVmax 6.1). Left paratracheal (4L) node mildly hypermetabolic (SUVmax 3.4, borderline). Right supraclavicular lymph node hypermetabolic (SUVmax 5.2, 1.3 cm). No hepatic lesions. No adrenal lesions. No osseous lesions. No pleural effusion. Impression: Stage IIIB non-small cell lung cancer with ipsilateral supraclavicular (N3) nodal involvement. No evidence of distant (M1) disease.

At age 63 years, the patient had an MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast performed at initial staging workup. Findings: No intracranial metastases identified. No leptomeningeal enhancement. No mass effect or midline shift. Ventricles and sulci are normal in caliber for age. Small scattered T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in the periventricular white matter consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease. Impression: No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease.

At age 63 years, the patient had next generation sequencing (FoundationOne CDx) performed for non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma, based on a specimen obtained from the primary right upper lobe mass via CT-guided core needle biopsy. Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 4 mutations/Mb (Low). Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). Actionable alterations: EGFR p.L858R (exon 21, pathogenic, allele frequency 38.2%). Co-mutations: TP53 p.R248W (exon 7, pathogenic, allele frequency 42.1%); SMARCA4 p.R1192H (exon 25, variant of uncertain significance); CDH1 p.E243K (variant of uncertain significance). Copy number alterations: MET amplification (copy number ratio 2.4, low-level, not meeting threshold for high amplification); CCND1 gain. No EGFR exon 19 deletion. No EGFR T790M detected. No KRAS mutation (consistent with EGFR-mutant status). No ALK, ROS1, RET, MET exon 14 skip, BRAF V600E, NTRK1/2/3 fusions identified. No ERBB2 amplification or mutation. Homologous recombination deficiency (HRD): Not detected. Relevant negative findings: No STK11, KEAP1, or NF1 mutations detected.

At age 63 years, the patient had a new patient oncology consultation. History and physical: Patient presents with a 3-month history of progressive cough, mild hemoptysis (two episodes), and 8-pound unintentional weight loss. She has exertional dyspnea but no chest pain. Performance status ECOG 1. On exam: Decreased breath sounds right apex, no pleural rub. Palpable right supraclavicular lymph node approximately 1 cm, firm, non-tender. No hepatomegaly. Discussion: Staging workup complete. Diagnosis of Stage IIIB NSCLC, adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R mutant. Multidisciplinary tumor board reviewed case. Given N3 (supraclavicular) nodal disease, patient is not a surgical candidate. Case discussed with radiation oncology. Given presence of targetable EGFR mutation (L858R), tumor board recommendation is to proceed with osimertinib monotherapy per LAURA trial paradigm and available evidence supporting EGFR TKI in EGFR-mutant stage III unresectable NSCLC. Risks and benefits discussed at length. Patient is agreeable. Cancer is present at this assessment.

At age 63 years, the patient initiated curative-intent (definitive) osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily as first-line therapy for Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma. Treatment rationale: EGFR-activating mutation (L858R), Stage IIIB unresectable disease. Concurrent chemoradiation was discussed but patient declined given toxicity concerns. Osimertinib chosen for its CNS penetration and superior efficacy over first-generation EGFR TKIs.

At age 63 years (approximately 3 weeks after osimertinib initiation), the patient developed grade 1-2 acneiform rash (paronychia right index finger, grade 1; facial papulopustular rash, grade 1) and grade 1 diarrhea (2-3 loose stools/day above baseline). Managed with topical clindamycin 1% solution to face, urea-based moisturizer, and dietary modifications. No dose reduction required. Rash improved to grade 1 by week 6. These are expected class-effect toxicities of EGFR TKI therapy.

At age 63 years (3 months after osimertinib initiation), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for restaging. Findings: Right upper lobe mass has decreased from 5.8 x 4.2 cm to 3.1 x 2.4 cm (RECIST 1.1: 47% decrease in longest diameter). Right hilar lymph nodes decreased in size (previously up to 1.9 cm, now 0.9 cm). Right paratracheal node (4R) decreased (now 0.7 cm, previously 1.4 cm). Right supraclavicular node no longer measurable on CT. No new lesions. No pleural effusion. No hepatic or adrenal lesions. Impression: Partial response to osimertinib per RECIST 1.1. Significant radiographic response in primary tumor and regional lymph nodes.

At age 63 years (3-month restaging), the patient had an oncology follow-up visit. She reports improved energy and resolution of hemoptysis. Cough is significantly improved. Weight has stabilized; she has regained 4 of the 8 pounds lost. Performance status ECOG 0-1. Review of imaging confirms partial response. No significant toxicity. Plan: Continue osimertinib 80 mg daily. Repeat imaging in 3 months. Cancer is present at this assessment but responding to therapy.

At age 63 years (6 months after osimertinib initiation), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for restaging. Findings: Right upper lobe mass has further decreased to 2.0 x 1.6 cm (RECIST 1.1: 65% decrease from baseline longest diameter). Right hilar lymph nodes remain small (0.6 cm). Previously enlarged mediastinal and supraclavicular nodes no longer pathologically enlarged. No new lesions. No hepatic, adrenal, or osseous lesions. Impression: Continued partial response to osimertinib, approaching near-complete response in lymph nodes. Primary tumor residual mass may represent treatment-related fibrosis versus residual viable tumor.

At age 64 years (12 months after osimertinib initiation), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for annual restaging. Findings: Right upper lobe residual mass stable at 1.8 x 1.5 cm (minimal change from 6-month scan). No interval new lesions in chest, abdomen, or pelvis. Right hilar and mediastinal lymph nodes remain within normal limits. No hepatic lesions. No new adrenal lesions. Impression: Stable partial response. No evidence of progressive disease. MRI brain also obtained: No intracranial lesions identified. Ventricles and sulci normal.

At age 64 years (14 months after osimertinib initiation), the patient had an urgent MRI brain with and without gadolinium for new neurological symptoms (headaches, mild word-finding difficulties). Findings: Three new enhancing lesions identified: (1) left frontal lobe, 12 mm, with surrounding vasogenic edema; (2) right cerebellar hemisphere, 8 mm, no significant edema; (3) left parietal lobe, 6 mm. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Mild mass effect from left frontal lesion without midline shift. Impression: New intracranial metastases (3 lesions), consistent with metastatic non-small cell lung carcinoma. Concurrent CT chest also obtained: Right upper lobe mass has increased from 1.8 cm to 2.7 cm (longest diameter), new right paratracheal lymph node 1.1 cm. Findings consistent with systemic progressive disease.

At age 64 years (14 months after osimertinib initiation, at time of progression), the patient underwent liquid biopsy (cell-free DNA, Guardant360 platform). Result: EGFR T790M resistance mutation detected (allele frequency 0.6%). EGFR L858R detected (allele frequency 1.2%). TP53 R248W detected (allele frequency 1.1%). No MET amplification detected (below threshold). No EGFR C797S detected. No PIK3CA mutation. No BRAF mutation. No RET fusion. Interpretation: Acquired EGFR T790M resistance mutation detected, consistent with acquired resistance to first/second-line EGFR TKI therapy. Note: Patient was on osimertinib (third-generation TKI), and T790M detection in this context is unusual. This may represent residual T790M clone (pre-existing) or a distinct resistance mechanism. Molecular tumor board consultation requested.

At age 64 years (14 months after osimertinib initiation), the patient had an urgent oncology visit for disease progression. She reports 3 weeks of worsening headaches, predominantly bifrontal, worse in the morning, partially responsive to acetaminophen. She also notes mild word-finding difficulty over the past 2 weeks but no focal weakness, vision changes, or seizures. Performance status ECOG 1. Exam: Oriented x3, mild anomic aphasia noted on testing, no focal motor deficits, no papilledema. CT chest confirms systemic progression. MRI brain confirms 3 new brain metastases. Liquid biopsy reveals acquired T790M (unusual with osimertinib; likely reflecting heterogeneous resistance mechanism). Osimertinib discontinued. Patient started on dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily for cerebral edema. Referral placed to radiation oncology for stereotactic radiosurgery evaluation. Multidisciplinary tumor board case reviewed. Plan: SRS to brain metastases followed by reassessment of systemic therapy. Given unusual T790M acquisition on osimertinib, likely multiple resistance mechanisms. Plan for tissue re-biopsy of primary lesion if feasible after SRS. Cancer present, progressing.

At age 64 years (approximately 6 weeks after progression diagnosis), the patient underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) using Gamma Knife Icon system for three intracranial metastases. Treatment details: (1) Left frontal lobe lesion (12 mm): 20 Gy in 1 fraction, prescription isodose 50%; (2) Right cerebellar hemisphere lesion (8 mm): 20 Gy in 1 fraction; (3) Left parietal lobe lesion (6 mm): 22 Gy in 1 fraction. Immobilization: Thermoplastic mask. Imaging guidance: MRI-based planning with gadolinium. No intraoperative complications. Patient tolerated procedure well. Post-SRS dexamethasone taper planned over 2 weeks.

At age 64 years (2 weeks post-SRS), the patient developed grade 1 radiation-related fatigue and transient worsening of headache in the immediate post-SRS period, consistent with expected post-treatment inflammatory response. Managed with continuation of dexamethasone taper and analgesics. Word-finding difficulties improved with steroid therapy. No seizures, no focal neurological deficits, no radiation necrosis requiring intervention at this time. She completed dexamethasone taper without neurological deterioration.

At age 64 years (8 weeks after progression, following SRS), the patient underwent CT-guided core needle re-biopsy of the enlarged right upper lobe mass and enlarged right paratracheal lymph node. Pathology result: Right upper lobe mass: Invasive adenocarcinoma, morphologically similar to original biopsy. IHC: TTF-1 positive, Napsin-A positive. Molecular testing (rapid PCR): EGFR L858R confirmed; EGFR T790M not detected by PCR (below detection threshold of 1%). Note: T790M may be present at low level as suggested by liquid biopsy. Full NGS re-profiling sent. Paratracheal lymph node: Metastatic adenocarcinoma, consistent with lung primary.

At age 64 years (re-biopsy at progression), the patient had next generation sequencing (FoundationOne CDx) performed on re-biopsy tissue from right upper lobe mass. Tumor mutational burden: 6 mutations/Mb (Low-intermediate). Microsatellite status: MSS. Actionable alterations: EGFR p.L858R (exon 21, pathogenic, allele frequency 31.4%). Resistance mutations: EGFR T790M detected at low allele frequency (3.1%, below standard threshold for high-level resistance but above background). EGFR C797S: Not detected. New alterations vs. baseline: PIK3CA p.E545K (exon 9, pathogenic, allele frequency 18.2%) -- newly acquired, not present on initial NGS; PTEN p.R130Q (exon 5, pathogenic) -- newly acquired. Previously identified co-mutations: TP53 p.R248W (persistent, allele frequency 35.1%); SMARCA4 p.R1192H (persistent). Copy number changes: MET amplification now high-level (copy number ratio 5.1, FISH confirmatory testing sent); CCND1 gain (persistent). No new fusions detected (ALK, ROS1, RET, NTRK1/2/3 all negative). Interpretation: Mixed/heterogeneous resistance to osimertinib with low-level T790M, newly acquired PIK3CA/PTEN pathway activation, and high-level MET amplification. This is consistent with polyclonal acquired resistance and represents a therapeutically challenging scenario.

At age 64 years (post-SRS, re-biopsy results available), the patient had an oncology visit for treatment planning. She reports improved headaches since SRS and dexamethasone taper completion. Performance status ECOG 1. She remains motivated and is interested in clinical trial participation. Repeat NGS reveals complex heterogeneous resistance to osimertinib: low-level T790M, MET amplification (high-level), and newly acquired PIK3CA/PTEN alterations. Discussed at molecular tumor board. Given MET amplification as likely dominant resistance driver, options discussed: (1) Platinum-based doublet chemotherapy as bridging therapy; (2) MET inhibitor combination; (3) Enrollment in clinical trial targeting MET-amplified NSCLC post-EGFR TKI progression. Decision: Initiate carboplatin/pemetrexed as bridging systemic therapy. Active enrollment in a Phase II trial of savolitinib plus osimertinib for MET-amplified EGFR-mutant NSCLC post-progression being pursued. Cancer present, progressing systemically but CNS disease under local treatment.

At age 64 years (approximately 10 weeks after progression diagnosis), the patient initiated palliative-intent carboplatin (AUC 5 IV day 1) plus pemetrexed (500 mg/m2 IV day 1) every 21 days as second-line systemic therapy for Stage IV (M1b brain metastases) EGFR L858R-mutant non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma, following acquired resistance to osimertinib. Vitamin B12 1000 mcg IM every 9 weeks and folic acid 400 mcg orally daily initiated as per pemetrexed protocol. Dexamethasone 4 mg orally twice daily on day -1, day 0, and day +1 of each cycle as pemetrexed antifolate prophylaxis.

At age 64 years (cycle 1-2 of carboplatin/pemetrexed), the patient developed grade 2 neutropenia (ANC nadir 1.1 x 10^9/L on day 14 of cycle 1) without fever or infection. Grade 1 fatigue. Grade 1 nausea managed with ondansetron 8 mg orally three times daily as needed. No grade 3 or 4 hematologic toxicities. No renal toxicity (creatinine stable). Cycle 2 administered on schedule without dose reduction. Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) prophylaxis (filgrastim) added starting cycle 2 given grade 2 neutropenia in cycle 1.

At age 64 years (after 3 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed, approximately 9 weeks after chemotherapy initiation), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain for restaging. CT chest: Right upper lobe mass has decreased from 2.7 cm to 2.1 cm. Right paratracheal node decreased from 1.1 cm to 0.7 cm. No new thoracic lesions. No hepatic or adrenal lesions. MRI brain: Left frontal lesion decreased from 12 mm to 7 mm, decreased surrounding edema. Right cerebellar lesion stable at 6 mm (decreased from 8 mm). Left parietal lesion now 3 mm (decreased from 6 mm). No new intracranial lesions. Impression: Partial response in chest (RECIST: 22% decrease in primary tumor). Continued response/stable disease in brain metastases post-SRS. No evidence of leptomeningeal disease.

At age 64 years (after 3 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed), the patient had an oncology follow-up visit. She reports tolerating chemotherapy reasonably well with manageable fatigue. Appetite is decreased. Weight loss of 3 pounds since starting chemotherapy. She is eager to transition to a clinical trial. Performance status ECOG 1. Imaging shows partial response in chest and stable/responding CNS disease. Clinical trial site contacted: She has been pre-screened for a Phase II investigator-sponsored trial of savolitinib (MET inhibitor) in combination with osimertinib rechallenge for MET-amplified, EGFR-mutant NSCLC post-third-generation TKI progression. She meets eligibility criteria pending final screening labs. Plan: Complete one additional cycle of carboplatin/pemetrexed (cycle 4), then transition to clinical trial. Cancer present, responding to current therapy.

At age 64 years (approximately 5 months after progression from osimertinib), the patient was enrolled and initiated treatment on a Phase II single-arm clinical trial: Savolitinib 300 mg orally once daily plus osimertinib 80 mg orally once daily for MET-amplified EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung carcinoma following progression on third-generation EGFR TKI. Carboplatin/pemetrexed discontinued after 4 cycles given transition to trial. Trial ID: NCT (investigator-sponsored, institutional protocol). Rationale: High-level MET amplification as dominant resistance mechanism identified on repeat NGS; combination addresses both MET bypass and residual EGFR-dependent signaling. Consent obtained; patient enrolled after 4 cycles of bridging chemotherapy.

At age 64 years (4 weeks after initiating savolitinib plus osimertinib clinical trial), the patient developed grade 2 peripheral edema (bilateral lower extremity, pitting, to mid-shin) consistent with savolitinib-related toxicity. Grade 1 nausea. Grade 1 fatigue. Acneiform rash returned to grade 1 consistent with osimertinib rechallenge. Peripheral edema managed with dietary sodium restriction and compression stockings; furosemide 20 mg orally daily was initiated. No dose interruption required. Edema improved to grade 1 over the following 3 weeks. Echocardiogram obtained: Normal LV function (EF 60-65%), no pericardial effusion.

At age 64 years (8 weeks after initiating savolitinib plus osimertinib clinical trial), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain for protocol-specified on-trial restaging. CT chest: Right upper lobe mass decreased from 2.1 cm to 1.4 cm (33% decrease from start of trial; 76% decrease from original baseline at diagnosis). Right paratracheal lymph node no longer measurable. No new lesions. No hepatic or adrenal disease. MRI brain: Left frontal lesion further decreased to 4 mm, no surrounding edema. Right cerebellar lesion 4 mm (stable). Left parietal lesion no longer visualized. No new intracranial lesions. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Impression: Partial response per RECIST 1.1 on trial. Favorable CNS response. Assessment: Best overall response to trial therapy is partial response. Patient continues trial therapy.

At age 64 years (8-week on-trial restaging), the patient had an on-trial oncology visit. She reports improved energy and reduced edema with furosemide. Weight is stable. She is tolerating the combination reasonably well. Performance status ECOG 1. Imaging confirms partial response; all lesions have decreased or resolved. Notably, the dominant MET-amplified resistant clone appears to be responding to savolitinib. She remains on full doses of both agents. Plan: Continue savolitinib plus osimertinib per protocol. Next restaging in 8 weeks. She is encouraged by the radiographic response. Cancer present, responding to clinical trial therapy. Molecular correlative samples (ctDNA, PBMC) collected per protocol.

At age 65 years (6 months after initiating savolitinib plus osimertinib clinical trial), the patient had protocol-specified restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain. CT chest: Right upper lobe residual mass stable at 1.3 cm. No new mediastinal or hilar adenopathy. No new lesions in chest, abdomen, or pelvis. MRI brain: Left frontal lesion now 2 mm (essentially resolved). Right cerebellar lesion stable at 4 mm. No new intracranial lesions. No leptomeningeal disease. Impression: Continued partial response/stable disease. No evidence of progressive disease at 6-month on-trial assessment. Patient is doing well clinically. Overall: Best RECIST response is partial response, maintained for 6+ months on trial.

At age 65 years (6-month on-trial assessment), the patient had a protocol visit and oncology assessment. She is tolerating savolitinib plus osimertinib well. Peripheral edema remains controlled on furosemide. Grade 1 fatigue and grade 1 rash are present but acceptable to patient. Performance status ECOG 1. She has returned to teaching part-time and reports acceptable quality of life. Imaging confirms ongoing partial response at 6 months on trial. ctDNA from most recent blood draw: EGFR L858R allele frequency declining (0.3%), MET amplification below detection threshold -- encouraging molecular response. Plan: Continue trial therapy. Next restaging in 8 weeks. Cancer present (residual disease), responding to therapy. Patient in ongoing partial response on Phase II clinical trial.

scenario_blurb: Stage IIIB non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) with EGFR L858R mutation in a 62-year-old former smoker. Initially treated with osimertinib with good partial response lasting 14 months, then develops progression with T790M resistance mutation and new brain metastases. Undergoes stereotactic radiosurgery and switches to platinum-based chemotherapy with eventual transition to clinical trial.
